reconstructed_sketch: From chapter context (2.67's loss-mechanism, 2.61's prior pratiṣṭhitā formulation, 2.58's tortoise verse), a verse at position 2.68 would probably teach the positive corollary of 2.67's prajñā-erosion mechanism. A competent writer would reach for 'when you control your senses, your judgment stabilises' or 'sense-restraint produces wisdom'. They would NOT, without seeing the verse, name the load-bearing tasmāt conjunction structurally, nor identify pratiṣṭhitā as the chapter's defining lexical thread, nor specify sarvaśaḥ as the totality-qualifier with 'on every side, sarvaśaḥ' verbatim retention.
verse_specific_signal: The 'mundane, not heroic' compression and the architecture-of-attention frame (PagerDuty severity-tuning, batched digests, DNS-layer blocks) is the verse's specific contribution at the operational layer — the verse names the structural condition under which the steady state stabilises, not the willpower-required-to-attain-it framing. This frame would not be reconstructible from chapter context alone; the chapter could equally support a willpower-framing reading (which would be wrong). The engineering layer's correct reading is licensed by the verse's specific structural-condition framing.
verdict_reason: Score 3 — chapter context provides general drift toward positive sense-control teaching but does not specify the architecture-of-attention frame, the cross-verse pratiṣṭhitā coherence, or the structural-condition rather than willpower stance. Pass.
